options.rationale: Correct. Retrieving and updating a whole value by a single unique key, with no need to query the contents, is the textbook key-value use case (carts, sessions, caching). It is the simplest and fastest model and is highly partitionable.

options.label: C
options.text: Column-family store
options.rationale: Wrong. Column-family stores excel at aggregation queries over columns for analytics and data warehousing, and are weak at heavy transactional updates. Fast whole-value lookups by key for cart/session data fit the key-value model instead.

options.label: D
options.text: Graph database
options.rationale: Wrong. Graph databases are for traversing relationships between many connected entities. A cart fetched by one user identifier has no relationship-traversal need, so the key-value model is the right and faster choice.

questions.stem: A ride-sharing company is building its big data pipeline and needs to bring driver-location updates and trip events into the platform continuously, the instant each event is produced, so that surge pricing can react within seconds. The team is choosing an ingestion tool specifically for this always-on, event-by-event feed. Which tool best fits this requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: Apache Kafka, because it is designed for real-time, event-driven ingestion that delivers data the moment it is generated
options.rationale: Correct. The scenario requires continuous, event-by-event ingestion with second-level reaction time, which is exactly what real-time ingestion provides. Apache Kafka is the streaming/event-ingestion tool suited to importing data immediately as it is generated, making it the right fit for surge pricing.

options.label: B
options.text: Apache NiFi, because it is the standard tool for scheduled, interval-based batch ingestion such as nightly loads
options.rationale: Incorrect. Apache NiFi is associated with batch ingestion that runs on a schedule or fixed interval (for example, a nightly load). The scenario explicitly needs data the instant it is produced for second-level reactions, so the real-time streaming tool Apache Kafka fits, not a batch tool.

options.label: C
options.text: Apache Atlas, because it ingests streaming events while also enforcing access policies on the data
options.rationale: Incorrect. Apache Atlas is a data governance tool used to enforce rules and policies for data access, quality, and usability; it is not an ingestion tool. The requirement here is continuous event ingestion, which is served by Apache Kafka.

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon S3, because it ingests live event streams directly as they occur
options.rationale: Incorrect. Amazon S3 is a distributed object storage service used in the storage layer to hold data across many nodes, not a real-time ingestion mechanism. Capturing events the moment they are generated is the job of the streaming ingestion tool Apache Kafka, after which data may be stored in a system like S3.

questions.stem: A team has one enormous 64 GB file in HDFS and wants many Map tasks to run on it in parallel rather than a single Map task reading the whole file. Which MapReduce internal concept makes this parallelism possible, and how does it work?

options.label: A
options.text: Input Splits: the file is divided into splits (default around 64 MB), and each split becomes the unit of work for one Map task, enabling many parallel map tasks over a single large file.
options.rationale: An Input Split is the unit of work for a single Map task (default about 64 MB); dividing the large file into splits lets multiple Map tasks run in parallel on one file, which is exactly the parallelism the team wants.

options.label: B
options.text: Output Format: it slices reducer output into pieces so each Map task can read one piece.
options.rationale: Output Format governs how reducers write their results, which happens after the map phase, not how a large input file is divided for parallel mappers. Input Splits provide that division.

options.label: C
options.text: Speculative execution: it launches duplicate Map tasks on the whole file to achieve parallelism.
options.rationale: Speculative execution runs backup copies of slow straggler tasks, not the mechanism that splits a file for parallel mapping. Input Splits define the parallel units of work.

options.label: D
options.text: The Partitioner: it cuts the input file into partitions, each handled by a separate Map task.
options.rationale: The Partitioner routes intermediate (K,V) pairs to reduce tasks; it does not divide the input file for mappers. Input Splits are what enable parallel map tasks on one large file.

questions.stem: A research lab currently runs its analytics on a single powerful server. As their dataset grows, they are debating two strategies: one engineer proposes replacing the existing server with a single, much more powerful and expensive machine that has more RAM and faster CPUs, while another proposes adding many additional inexpensive commodity machines that work on the problem in parallel. The second engineer notes that this second approach is the philosophy underpinning Hadoop. Which statement correctly identifies these two strategies and explains why Hadoop favors the second?

options.label: A
options.text: Replacing the server with one more powerful machine is 'scaling out,' and adding many commodity machines is 'scaling up'; Hadoop favors scaling up because a single large machine is easier to administer.
options.rationale: This reverses the definitions: scaling UP means upgrading to a more powerful single machine, and scaling OUT means adding more nodes. It also misstates Hadoop's preference. The correct answer keeps the definitions straight and explains that Hadoop favors scaling out across commodity hardware for parallelism and cost.

options.label: B
options.text: Replacing the server with one more powerful machine is 'scaling up,' and adding many commodity machines is 'scaling out'; Hadoop favors scaling out because it adds parallel processing capacity using inexpensive commodity hardware rather than relying on one costly machine.
options.rationale: Correct. Scaling up replaces equipment with a faster/more efficient single machine, while scaling out adds more workers (nodes) that run in parallel. Hadoop is built to scale out horizontally across clusters of inexpensive commodity hardware, distributing storage and processing rather than depending on one expensive server.

options.label: C
options.text: Both strategies describe 'scaling up'; the only difference is the price of the hardware, and Hadoop favors whichever option is cheaper at the moment of purchase.
options.rationale: Both strategies are not the same thing: upgrading one machine (scale-up) and adding many machines (scale-out) are distinct approaches. Hadoop's preference is architectural (horizontal scalability via parallel commodity nodes), not a moment-to-moment price comparison. The correct answer distinguishes the two and ties Hadoop to scaling out.

options.label: D
options.text: Adding many commodity machines is 'scaling out,' but Hadoop actually favors scaling up because distributing work across many nodes introduces too much network overhead to ever be worthwhile.
options.rationale: This correctly labels scale-out but wrongly claims Hadoop favors scaling up. Hadoop is fundamentally designed for horizontal scalability (scaling out) on commodity clusters, mitigating network costs through data locality. The correct answer states Hadoop favors scaling out for parallel capacity on inexpensive hardware.

questions.stem: A team is mapping the responsibilities of Hadoop's three core architectural components for a new hire. They want a one-line description of each. Which assignment of HDFS, YARN, and MapReduce to their roles is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: HDFS handles resource management, YARN provides distributed storage, and MapReduce names files in the cluster.
options.rationale: These roles are scrambled. HDFS is the storage layer and YARN is resource management; naming files is part of the file system, not a separate component, so this mapping is wrong.

options.label: B
options.text: HDFS is the computing engine, YARN is the distributed filesystem, and MapReduce manages cluster resources.
options.rationale: Each component is mislabeled. HDFS stores rather than computes, YARN manages resources rather than stores, and MapReduce computes rather than manages resources.

options.label: C
options.text: All three are interchangeable layers that each independently provide storage, scheduling, and computation.
options.rationale: The three components have distinct, complementary roles; they are not interchangeable. HDFS is storage, YARN is resources, and MapReduce is processing.

options.label: D
options.text: HDFS is the distributed filesystem for storage, YARN handles resource management, and MapReduce is the computing engine/programming model for processing.
options.rationale: This is the correct division of Hadoop's core: HDFS stores data, YARN manages cluster resources, and MapReduce is the computation engine and programming model for processing that data.

questions.stem: A 500 MB file is written to an HDFS cluster configured with a 128 MB block size and the default replication factor. Approximately how many blocks will the file be split into, and how many total block copies will the cluster store across DataNodes?

options.label: A
options.text: 1 block stored as 1 copy, because HDFS stores each file whole and does not replicate by default.
options.rationale: HDFS splits large files into blocks and replicates each three times by default. A 500 MB file cannot be a single 128 MB block, and replication is not disabled by default.

options.label: B
options.text: 4 blocks stored as 12 total copies, since 500 MB divides into four 128 MB blocks and each block is replicated three times by default.
options.rationale: 500 MB / 128 MB yields four blocks (three full plus one partial), and the default replication factor of 3 means each block has three copies, giving 4 x 3 = 12 total block copies across DataNodes.

options.label: C
options.text: 4 blocks stored as 4 copies, because HDFS keeps exactly one copy of each block to save space.
options.rationale: The block count of 4 is right, but HDFS replicates blocks three times by default for fault tolerance, not once. The total is 12 copies, not 4.

options.label: D
options.text: 64 blocks stored as 64 copies, because HDFS always uses a fixed 8 MB block size with no replication.
options.rationale: HDFS default block sizes are 64 MB or 128 MB, not 8 MB, and replication is 3 by default. With a 128 MB block size the file is 4 blocks with 12 copies.

questions.stem: A research team receives daily exports from 30 partner hospitals. Each partner sends differently shaped records, and partners frequently add or rename fields without notice. The team wants to land every export the moment it arrives, with zero upfront agreement on a fixed table structure, and only impose a query structure later when a specific analysis is run. If they instead loaded everything into a traditional relational SQL database, what is the core obstacle they would hit, and which approach avoids it?

options.label: A
options.text: A traditional SQL database enforces schema-on-write, requiring a rigid structure to be defined before any record can be inserted, which the changing partner fields would constantly break; a data lake stores the files first and applies schema-on-read only when queried, avoiding the obstacle.
options.rationale: Correct. A relational database demands a fixed schema at insert time (schema-on-write), so unpredictable, evolving partner fields would repeatedly violate the predefined columns. A data lake lands raw files first and applies structure only at read time (schema-on-read), which is exactly what the team wants.

options.label: B
options.text: A traditional SQL database cannot store text or JSON data at all, so the exports would be rejected outright; a data lake is the only system capable of holding semi-structured content.
options.rationale: Incorrect. Relational databases can store text and even JSON content; the real obstacle is the schema-on-write requirement that a structure be defined before insertion, not an inability to hold the data. The correct answer identifies the rigid up-front schema as the true blocker that schema-on-read avoids.

options.label: C
options.text: A traditional SQL database would be too cheap and too scalable for this volume, so the team should add a data lake purely to raise storage costs and slow ingestion.
options.rationale: Incorrect and backwards. Object-storage data lakes are the low-cost, highly scalable option, and the goal is faster, more flexible ingestion, not higher cost. The actual obstacle with SQL here is its mandatory predefined schema, which the schema-on-read data lake sidesteps.

options.label: D
options.text: A traditional SQL database applies schema-on-read just like a data lake, so the only difference is the file format, and either choice would work identically for these evolving exports.
options.rationale: Incorrect. Traditional SQL uses schema-on-write (structure defined before insertion), not schema-on-read, so it would not work identically. The correct answer hinges precisely on this difference: only the schema-on-read data lake tolerates the constantly changing partner fields.

questions.stem: An operations team needs to reliably collect and aggregate massive streams of web-server log files generated across many machines and continuously move them into HDFS for later analysis. Which ecosystem tool is specifically designed for this log-ingestion workload?

options.label: A
options.text: Hive, because it ingests raw log streams into HDFS through HQL commands.
options.rationale: Hive provides SQL-like (HQL) querying of data already in HDFS by converting queries to MapReduce; it is not a log-collection pipeline. Flume is the log-ingestion tool.

options.label: B
options.text: Pig, because its Pig Latin scripts continuously stream log files from servers into HDFS.
options.rationale: Pig is a framework for running MapReduce on HDFS via Pig Latin scripts, used for data processing, not for reliably collecting and moving log streams. Flume handles log ingestion.

options.label: C
options.text: Avro, since it streams and ingests log files directly into HDFS in real time.
options.rationale: Avro is a data serialization system for marshaling data and JSON interchange (with RPC), not a log-collection pipeline. Flume is the tool built to ingest logs into HDFS.

options.label: D
options.text: Flume, a reliable distributed system for collecting, aggregating, and moving large volumes of log data into HDFS.
options.rationale: Flume is precisely a reliable distributed system for collecting, aggregating, and moving large log data into HDFS, matching the team's continuous log-ingestion requirement.

questions.stem: A government technology advisor is preparing a briefing on why big data became practical and widespread in the last two decades. She wants to list the key drivers that enabled its rise. Which set best represents the recognized key drivers of big data?

options.label: A
options.text: The decline of the internet, shrinking sensor deployments, abandonment of cloud computing, and rising storage costs
options.rationale: Each item is the opposite of an actual driver; the real drivers include the explosion of digital content, growth of IoT, advances in cloud computing, and falling storage costs, as the correct option lists.

options.label: B
options.text: Explosion of digital content, growth of IoT and sensors, advances in distributed and cloud computing, and cost reduction in storage and processing
options.rationale: These are the recognized key drivers of big data: more digital content, proliferating IoT/sensors, better distributed/cloud computing, and cheaper storage and processing.

options.label: C
options.text: Mandatory paper record-keeping, slower processors, and government bans on data collection
options.rationale: These would impede rather than enable big data. The genuine drivers are technological growth and falling costs, as captured in the correct answer.

options.label: D
options.text: A single driver: the invention of the relational database in the 1970s
options.rationale: Relational databases relate to traditional data processing, not the modern drivers of big data. The correct answer lists multiple contemporary drivers like IoT, cloud, and cheap storage.

questions.stem: A fraud-detection team must rapidly answer questions like 'find accounts within three transaction hops of a known fraudulent account' and 'show all entities that share a device and an address.' These queries traverse many-to-many relationships and would require numerous expensive JOINs in a relational database. Which NoSQL model is purpose-built for this and why?

options.label: A
options.text: Key-value store, because hops can be encoded as keys
options.rationale: Wrong. Key-value stores only retrieve values by key and cannot traverse relationships. Multi-hop relationship queries need a model that physically captures connections, which is the graph database.

options.label: B
options.text: Document store, because each account is a self-contained document
options.rationale: Wrong. Self-contained documents are great for flexible per-entity data, but traversing many-to-many connections across entities is not their strength. Graph databases store relationships as first-class edges, making multi-hop traversal fast.

options.label: C
options.text: Graph database, because relationships are stored as edges so multi-hop traversals are fast without JOINs
options.rationale: Correct. Graph databases treat relationships (edges) as first-class, physically capturing connections between nodes. Traversing relationships is therefore lightning fast and avoids expensive JOIN calculations, exactly what multi-hop fraud queries need.

options.label: D
options.text: Column-family store, because it aggregates transaction columns quickly
options.rationale: Wrong. Column-family stores are optimized for columnar aggregation (SUM, COUNT, AVG), not relationship traversal. Finding accounts within N hops requires edge traversal, which is the graph database's specialty.

questions.stem: A streaming-media company adopts a distributed NoSQL database for its 'recently watched' feature and is told the system follows the BASE model rather than strict ACID. An engineer summarizes for the team what they should expect from BASE behavior in this deployment. All of the following are accurate expectations of a BASE system EXCEPT which one?

options.label: A
options.text: The database stays basically available and keeps responding to requests even while some nodes are out of sync.
options.rationale: This IS an accurate expectation. The 'BA' in BASE stands for Basically Available, meaning the system prioritizes staying operational and responsive over refusing requests during trouble. This belongs to BASE, unlike the exception, which describes a strict ACID guarantee that BASE deliberately relaxes.

options.label: B
options.text: After a write, replicas may briefly disagree, but if no new updates arrive the data will converge to a consistent state over time.
options.rationale: This IS an accurate expectation. The 'E' in BASE stands for Eventual consistency: replicas reconcile and converge given enough time without new writes. This is core to BASE, whereas the exception wrongly demands the immediate, instant-consistency guarantee that BASE trades away.

options.label: C
options.text: The system tolerates a 'soft state' where stored data may change over time even without new input, as replicas reconcile in the background.
options.rationale: This IS an accurate expectation. The 'S' in BASE stands for Soft state, acknowledging that the data view can shift on its own as background replication and reconciliation proceed. This belongs to BASE; the exception instead describes the rigid, all-or-nothing instantaneous consistency BASE relaxes.

options.label: D
options.text: Every read anywhere in the cluster is guaranteed to reflect the most recent write the instant it commits, with no possibility of stale data.
options.rationale: This is the EXCEPTION (the false statement). Guaranteeing that every read instantly reflects the latest commit is strict linearizable consistency, an ACID-style promise. BASE deliberately gives this up in favor of being Basically Available, accepting Soft state and Eventual consistency, so stale reads are explicitly possible until replicas converge.

questions.stem: An e-commerce platform needs to capture clickstream events and act on them the moment they occur to power live recommendations, while it also pulls a full copy of yesterday's warehouse inventory once each night. Which ingestion strategy and example tooling best matches each need?

options.label: A
options.text: Use batch ingestion (e.g., Apache Kafka) for the live clicks and real-time ingestion (e.g., Apache NiFi) for the nightly inventory pull
options.rationale: The tools and modes are swapped: live clicks need real-time ingestion (Kafka) and a scheduled nightly pull is batch (NiFi). This option reverses both assignments.

options.label: B
options.text: Use real-time ingestion (e.g., Apache Kafka) for the live clickstream and batch ingestion (e.g., Apache NiFi) for the scheduled nightly inventory pull
options.rationale: Real-time ingestion processes data immediately as generated and is well served by Apache Kafka, fitting the live clickstream, while batch ingestion runs on a schedule/interval (nightly) and is well served by Apache NiFi for the inventory copy.

options.label: C
options.text: Use batch ingestion for both feeds because all ingestion should be scheduled to reduce load
options.rationale: Live recommendations require immediate, real-time ingestion of clicks; batching them would delay action. The correct answer reserves batch only for the scheduled nightly pull.

options.label: D
options.text: Use real-time ingestion for both feeds because real-time is always superior to batch
options.rationale: A nightly full inventory copy is inherently a scheduled, interval-based job suited to batch ingestion; real-time is not universally superior, as the correct answer recognizes by matching each mode to its need.

questions.stem: A new engineer is reviewing the advantages that Hadoop offers over a traditional centralized enterprise system. All of the following are genuine advantages of Hadoop EXCEPT which one?

options.label: A
options.text: It runs on inexpensive commodity hardware and does not require RAID storage arrays.
options.rationale: This IS a real Hadoop advantage: it uses inexpensive commodity hardware with no RAID needed, so it does not belong as the exception.

options.label: B
options.text: It requires expensive proprietary tape-backup systems to guarantee data resilience.
options.rationale: This is the exception: Hadoop achieves resilience through replication and explicitly does NOT need tape backups, and the software is free, so requiring expensive tape backups is false.

options.label: C
options.text: It provides resilience through data replication rather than relying on backups.
options.rationale: This IS a genuine advantage: Hadoop's replication provides resilience without tape backups, so it correctly belongs in the list of advantages.

options.label: D
options.text: It enables massive parallelism and locality of execution using free software.
options.rationale: This IS a real advantage: Hadoop offers massive parallelism, locality of execution, and free software, so it is not the exception.

questions.stem: A company already operates a Hadoop cluster managed by YARN and wants to deploy Spark jobs without introducing a new resource manager. Which statement about Spark's cluster manager options correctly supports this decision?

options.label: A
options.text: Spark can run on Hadoop YARN, the standard Hadoop scheduler, so it can reuse the existing cluster manager
options.rationale: Correct. The brief lists Hadoop YARN as one of Spark's scheduler options and identifies it as the standard Hadoop scheduler, so the company can deploy Spark on its existing YARN.

options.label: B
options.text: Spark requires its Standalone Scheduler and cannot integrate with an existing YARN deployment
options.rationale: Wrong. Spark is not limited to its Standalone Scheduler; it can also use YARN or Mesos. The company can reuse YARN rather than being forced onto Standalone.

options.label: C
options.text: Spark only runs on Mesos, so the company must replace YARN with Mesos
options.rationale: Wrong. Mesos is just one supported option; YARN and the Standalone Scheduler are also supported. No replacement of YARN is required.

options.label: D
options.text: Spark embeds its own resource manager into HDFS, so the choice of cluster manager is irrelevant
options.rationale: Wrong. Spark does not embed a manager into HDFS; it uses an external scheduler (YARN, Mesos, or Standalone). Here YARN is the natural fit for the existing setup.

questions.stem: A logistics company has a finalized, rarely-changing shipments dataset with 150 columns stored in a cloud data lake. Their main workload is a nightly batch job that runs heavy analytical aggregations touching only 4 or 5 columns at a time across billions of rows. An engineer wants to commit to a single machine-optimized binary file format. A teammate argues for a row-based binary format because 'binary is fast and it writes records efficiently.' Which format should the company choose, and why is the teammate's reasoning flawed for this workload?

options.label: A
options.text: Parquet, because its columnar layout lets the engine read only the few columns each query needs and skip the other ~145, so the row-based format's per-record write speed is irrelevant to a read-heavy, few-column analytical workload.
options.rationale: Correct. With a stable schema and queries touching only a handful of the 150 columns over billions of rows, a columnar binary format (Parquet) isolates and reads just the required columns and bypasses the rest, minimizing I/O. The teammate fixates on row-based write efficiency, but this workload is read-heavy analytics on a tiny fraction of columns, where column pruning matters far more than how records are written.

options.label: B
options.text: Avro, because it is a machine-optimized binary format and the teammate is right that row-based binary formats are the fastest choice for any large analytical aggregation.
options.rationale: Wrong. Avro is row-based, so an aggregation touching only 4-5 columns still forces the engine to read every field of every record, creating massive unnecessary I/O. Avro shines for streaming and frequent schema evolution, neither of which applies to this stable, read-heavy batch workload. Parquet's columnar pruning is what actually fits here.

options.label: C
options.text: CSV, because keeping the data human-readable makes the few-column aggregations easier for the engine to scan and avoids any binary-format complexity.
options.rationale: Wrong. CSV is a row-based text format with no schema and is highly inefficient at big-data scale; the engine must still read every field of every row, and parsing text is slower than binary. Human readability does not speed up analytical scans. Parquet's compressed, columnar, schema-aware design is far better for billions of rows with few-column queries.

options.label: D
options.text: Avro for storage plus CSV exports, because pairing a row-based binary format with a text format covers both write speed and readability and makes column-subset queries fast.
options.rationale: Wrong. Both Avro and CSV are row-based, so neither enables column pruning; queries hitting 4-5 of 150 columns would still read entire records, and maintaining two formats adds overhead without solving the I/O problem. The workload calls for a single columnar binary format like Parquet that reads only the needed columns.

questions.stem: A study group lists facts about RDDs and how Spark handles their storage and recovery. All of the following statements about RDDs are TRUE EXCEPT which one?

options.label: A
options.text: RDDs are distributed collections of objects spread across cluster nodes and split into partitions
options.rationale: This statement is TRUE, so it is not the exception. The brief defines RDDs as distributed collections of objects spread across nodes and split into partitions.

options.label: B
options.text: When there is not enough memory, RDD data may be stored on local disk instead of main memory
options.rationale: This statement is TRUE, so it is not the exception. The brief says RDDs are kept in executors' main memory when possible, or on local disk if memory is insufficient.

options.label: C
options.text: Spark can recompute lost RDD data after executor failures using tracked lineage information
options.rationale: This statement is TRUE, so it is not the exception. The brief states Spark tracks lineage to efficiently recompute lost data due to executor failures.

options.label: D
options.text: Once constructed, an RDD's content can be edited in place by any executor that holds its partition
options.rationale: Correct (this is the EXCEPTION / false statement). RDDs are immutable once constructed; their content cannot be modified. The other three statements are accurate per the brief.

questions.stem: A bank stores incoming registration files exactly as received from the source system, with no cleaning applied. A separate team then produces a standardized, deduplicated customer-account table by removing invalid records and joining sources. Finally, a reporting team builds an aggregated dashboard of account-risk indicators. In the Medallion architecture, which layers do these three datasets correspond to, in order?

options.label: A
options.text: Bronze (raw as received), Silver (cleaned and joined), Gold (business-ready aggregated)
options.rationale: Bronze holds raw data exactly as received, Silver holds cleaned/joined/standardized data with invalid records removed, and Gold holds business-ready aggregated data for reporting. This matches the three datasets in order.

options.label: B
options.text: Gold (raw as received), Silver (cleaned and joined), Bronze (business-ready aggregated)
options.rationale: This reverses Bronze and Gold. Raw data belongs in Bronze and the aggregated reporting dataset belongs in Gold, not the other way around.

options.label: C
options.text: Silver (raw as received), Bronze (cleaned and joined), Gold (business-ready aggregated)
options.rationale: This swaps Bronze and Silver. Raw, uncleaned data is the Bronze layer; the cleaned and joined table is the Silver layer.

options.label: D
options.text: Bronze (raw as received), Gold (cleaned and joined), Silver (business-ready aggregated)
options.rationale: This swaps Silver and Gold. The cleaned/joined table is Silver and the aggregated reporting dataset is Gold, so the labels for the second and third datasets are reversed.

questions.stem: A media company stores a 150-column events table in its data lake and runs two very different workloads against it. Workload 1 is an analytics job that computes SELECT SUM(revenue) FROM events scanning billions of rows but touching only one column. Workload 2 is a streaming ingestion pipeline whose upstream schema gains new fields almost weekly, and the team needs a binary format optimized for writing whole records as they continuously arrive with evolving schemas. The team wants to pick the single best-fit binary format for EACH workload. Which pairing is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Use Parquet for the analytics aggregation and Avro for the evolving-schema streaming ingestion.
options.rationale: Correct. Parquet is a columnar binary format that isolates and reads only the single needed column, minimizing I/O for an aggregation over billions of rows, which is ideal for Workload 1. Avro is a row-based binary format purpose-built for data streaming and continuous schema evolution, making it the natural fit for Workload 2's frequently changing upstream schema.

options.label: B
options.text: Use Avro for the analytics aggregation and Parquet for the evolving-schema streaming ingestion.
options.rationale: Incorrect because the roles are reversed. Avro is row-based, so an aggregation touching one column would still pull every field per record, defeating the I/O savings; Parquet's columnar layout is what makes the aggregation efficient. Likewise, columnar Parquet is less suited to continuous record-by-record streaming with constant schema evolution, which is exactly Avro's strength. The correct pairing assigns Parquet to analytics and Avro to streaming.

options.label: C
options.text: Use CSV for the analytics aggregation and JSON for the evolving-schema streaming ingestion.
options.rationale: Incorrect because both are human-readable text formats that fail at scale, not machine-optimized binary formats. CSV is row-based and inefficient for big-data analytical queries, and JSON produces massive files with very slow analytical queries. The question explicitly asks for binary formats; Parquet (columnar) and Avro (row-based, schema-evolving) are the right binary choices.

options.label: D
options.text: Use ORC for both workloads, since a single columnar Hadoop format can serve analytics and streaming equally well.
options.rationale: Incorrect because ORC is a columnar binary format tuned for Hadoop/Hive analytics, so while it could serve the aggregation, it is not the row-based format built for continuous streaming ingestion with frequent schema evolution. Standardizing on one columnar format ignores that streaming favors a row-based, schema-evolving format; Avro fits Workload 2 far better than ORC, so Parquet plus Avro is the stronger pairing.

questions.stem: An analytics team migrated from a relational warehouse to a NoSQL store and is now struggling to run ad-hoc reports that join data scattered across many different aggregates, something their old SQL system did easily. They also note each NoSQL product they evaluated had its own unfamiliar API. Which pair of NoSQL drawbacks does this experience reflect?

options.label: A
options.text: The analytics gap (limited native complex/ad-hoc JOIN queries) and lack of standardization (each database has its own API)
options.rationale: Correct. The difficulty running ad-hoc joins reflects NoSQL's analytics gap (limited native complex JOIN capability), and the unfamiliar per-product interfaces reflect the lack of standardization (each NoSQL database has its own API and learning curve).

options.label: B
options.text: Weak schema flexibility and vertical-only scaling
options.rationale: Wrong. NoSQL actually offers strong schema flexibility and horizontal scaling; these are advantages, not the drawbacks described. The pains here are the analytics/JOIN gap and the lack of a standard API.

options.label: C
options.text: Strict ACID overhead and a single point of failure
options.rationale: Wrong. NoSQL typically relaxes ACID (favoring eventual consistency) and avoids single points of failure via replication. The experience described is about poor ad-hoc joins and inconsistent APIs, not ACID overhead or SPOF.

options.label: D
options.text: Inability to handle large data volume and poor availability
options.rationale: Wrong. Handling large volume and providing high availability are NoSQL strengths, not weaknesses. The actual drawbacks shown are the analytics/JOIN gap and lack of standardization across APIs.

questions.stem: A data engineering team is cleaning a customer dataset. They (1) remove records with clearly wrong birthdates, (2) append each customer's region by joining a geographic reference table, (3) convert all dates into a single consistent 'YYYY-MM-DD' representation, and (4) parse free-text address blobs into discrete street, city, and ZIP fields. Which transformation name correctly labels each of these four steps, respectively?

options.label: A
options.text: Cleaning, enrichment, normalization, structuring
options.rationale: Removing/correcting inaccurate records is cleaning, adding context like region is enrichment, standardizing date format is normalization, and breaking free-text into discrete fields is structuring, matching all four steps in order.

options.label: B
options.text: Normalization, cleaning, structuring, enrichment
options.rationale: Removing wrong records is cleaning (not normalization), and adding region is enrichment (not the last step). Multiple labels are misordered relative to the correct mapping.

options.label: C
options.text: Enrichment, cleaning, structuring, normalization
options.rationale: Removing bad records is cleaning (not enrichment) and standardizing the date format is normalization (not the third step). The first and other labels are misassigned here.

options.label: D
options.text: Cleaning, normalization, enrichment, structuring
options.rationale: The first step is correctly cleaning, but this option places normalization second and enrichment third; adding region by joining a reference table is enrichment and belongs second, while standardizing the date format is normalization and belongs third, so the middle two are swapped.

questions.stem: An architect is documenting the standard layers of a big data architecture from the point where raw data first arrives to where insights are delivered. She wants the layers placed in the correct functional order. Which ordering is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Analytics and servicing, then storage and processing, then ingestion, then data sources
options.rationale: This reverses the flow. Data must originate at sources, be ingested, then stored and processed, and only then analyzed and served, as the correct option orders it.

options.label: B
options.text: Data sources, then data ingestion, then storage and processing, then analytics and servicing, with governance/security/monitoring overarching all layers
options.rationale: This is the documented layered architecture: sources feed ingestion, which loads into storage and processing, then analytics and servicing deliver insights, while governance, security, and monitoring span across every layer.

options.label: C
options.text: Data ingestion, then data sources, then analytics, then storage
options.rationale: Ingestion cannot precede the sources it pulls from, and storage cannot follow analytics. The correct order places sources first and storage before analytics.

options.label: D
options.text: Governance and security must be the first sequential step that finishes before ingestion can ever start
options.rationale: Governance, security, and monitoring are overarching concerns that span all layers, not a single first step that completes before ingestion, as the correct answer clarifies.

questions.stem: A payment-processing application requires that once a money-transfer transaction commits, every subsequent read anywhere reflects it immediately, and partial transactions must never be visible. A separate social-feed application instead prioritizes staying responsive and is comfortable if some users briefly see slightly stale data that converges later. Which pairing of transaction philosophies best fits these two applications respectively?

options.label: A
options.text: Payment app: BASE; Social feed: ACID
options.rationale: Wrong. The pairing is reversed. The payment app's need for immediate, all-or-nothing consistency is ACID; the social feed's tolerance for temporary staleness that converges is BASE.

options.label: B
options.text: Payment app: ACID; Social feed: BASE
options.rationale: Correct. The payment app needs strict, immediate consistency and all-or-nothing transactions, which is ACID. The social feed prioritizes availability and accepts soft state with eventual consistency, which is BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency).

options.label: C
options.text: Both applications require ACID because both involve user data
options.rationale: Wrong. Involving user data does not mandate ACID. The social feed explicitly accepts stale-then-converging reads to stay responsive, which is the BASE model, not ACID.

options.label: D
options.text: Both applications fit BASE because both are modern web applications
options.rationale: Wrong. Being a modern web app does not force BASE. The payment app's requirement that committed transfers be immediately visible everywhere with no partial states is the ACID model, not BASE's eventual consistency.

questions.stem: A startup must process a 50 TB dataset that is far larger than any single server's disk. They want an open-source platform that provides distributed storage, distributed processing, and resilience to hardware failures while running on inexpensive commodity machines. Which description correctly characterizes Apache Hadoop as a fit for these needs?

options.label: A
options.text: A proprietary, single-server database engine optimized for low-latency transactional queries on small datasets.
options.rationale: Hadoop is open-source, runs across clusters of commodity machines, and is built for large-scale distributed batch work, not a proprietary single-server transactional engine for small data.

options.label: B
options.text: A closed-source in-memory cache designed to keep an entire dataset in RAM for sub-millisecond access.
options.rationale: Hadoop is open-source and disk-oriented for very large datasets that cannot fit in RAM; it is not an in-memory cache. The startup's 50 TB clearly cannot live in memory.

options.label: C
options.text: An open-source framework that runs applications on large clusters of commodity hardware, providing distributed storage, distributed processing, and fault tolerance.
options.rationale: This matches Hadoop exactly: an open-source big-data platform running on commodity clusters with distributed storage, distributed processing, and fault tolerance, ideal for a dataset that exceeds a single machine.

options.label: D
options.text: A specialized GPU-only computation library that requires RAID-protected enterprise storage arrays to operate.
options.rationale: Hadoop uses commodity hardware and needs no RAID, relying on replication for resilience. It is a general distributed framework, not a GPU-only library tied to enterprise RAID arrays.

questions.stem: A financial-services company runs its data lake on cheap cloud object storage and is evaluating adding a lakehouse table format (such as Delta Lake or Apache Iceberg) on top of those files. A consultant lists the benefits the team should expect to gain by adopting this layer. All of the following are genuine capabilities a lakehouse table format adds on top of raw object storage EXCEPT which one?

options.label: A
options.text: ACID transactions, so concurrent writes never leave a table in a half-updated, inconsistent state.
options.rationale: This IS a real lakehouse capability. Table formats like Delta Lake and Iceberg add ACID transaction guarantees on top of object storage, which is precisely one of the reliability features that plain file storage lacks, so it does belong on the list.

options.label: B
options.text: Time travel and versioning, so auditors can reproduce exactly what a table looked like at an earlier point in time.
options.rationale: This IS a real lakehouse capability. Versioning/time travel lets teams query historical snapshots for auditing and rollback, a feature explicitly provided by lakehouse table formats over raw files, so it belongs on the list.

options.label: C
options.text: Row-level updates and deletes, so individual records can be corrected without rewriting an entire dataset by hand.
options.rationale: This IS a real lakehouse capability. Table formats add fine-grained update and delete support, which raw append-only object storage does not natively offer, so it correctly belongs on the list.

options.label: D
options.text: Elimination of the need for cloud object storage entirely, replacing buckets with a self-managed on-premise block-storage cluster.
options.rationale: This is the EXCEPTION and the false claim. A lakehouse table format is layered ON TOP OF cheap, scalable cloud object storage (S3, ADLS, GCS); it deliberately keeps that storage rather than replacing it, and it certainly does not push teams back toward self-managed on-premise clusters. The genuine additions are ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time travel/versioning, and row-level updates/deletes (options A, B, and C), so D is the one that does not belong.

questions.stem: A research team needs to analyze a large social network represented as a graph: they want to find connected subgraphs of users and compute influence rankings for accounts using a PageRank-style algorithm, all running in parallel on their existing Spark cluster. Which component of the Spark stack is purpose-built to handle this kind of workload?

options.label: A
options.text: GraphX, the Spark library dedicated to graph processing, which supports tasks such as subgraph searching and PageRank.
options.rationale: Correct. GraphX is the Spark stack component built specifically for graph processing, and its capabilities include subgraph searching and computing rankings like PageRank, which is exactly what this team needs.

options.label: B
options.text: MLlib, because finding subgraphs and ranking accounts are clustering and classification problems handled by the machine-learning library.
options.rationale: Wrong. MLlib provides parallel machine-learning algorithms such as clustering, classification, and dimensional-reduction preprocessing, not graph-specific operations. Subgraph searching and PageRank are graph-processing tasks served by GraphX, the correct answer.

options.label: C
options.text: Spark SQL, since the network can be stored as structured tables and queried with SQL to traverse relationships and rank nodes.
options.rationale: Wrong. Spark SQL is for querying structured datasets via SQL or query APIs over sources like Hive tables, Parquet, and JSON; it is not designed for graph traversal or PageRank. GraphX is the purpose-built graph component, making it the correct choice.

options.label: D
options.text: Spark Streaming, because analyzing a continuously growing social network is inherently a real-time stream-processing task.
options.rationale: Wrong. Spark Streaming processes live data streams in real time, but the scenario describes analyzing an existing large graph, not ingesting a live stream. The graph-specific operations of subgraph search and PageRank belong to GraphX, the correct answer.

questions.stem: An analyst loads a 50 GB log dataset once and then runs dozens of ad-hoc exploratory queries against it over the next hour, expecting near-interactive response times. Which approach matches Spark's documented handling of this 'read once, query many times' situation?

options.label: A
options.text: Re-reading the full dataset from HDFS at the start of every query to guarantee the freshest data
options.rationale: Wrong. Re-reading from disk for each query is the slow MapReduce-style behavior Spark avoids. The correct approach reads once and serves later queries from distributed memory.

options.label: B
options.text: Reading the data once from HDFS and storing it in distributed memory split across each server's memory, then querying it repeatedly from memory
options.rationale: Correct. The brief describes exactly this pattern for multiple analyses of the same data: read once from HDFS, store in distributed memory, and query repeatedly, which delivers the interactive response the analyst wants.

options.label: C
options.text: Running each query as a separate batch MapReduce job that writes intermediate output to HDFS
options.rationale: Wrong. Per-query batch jobs with HDFS intermediate writes reintroduce heavy disk I/O. Spark instead caches the data once in memory for fast repeated querying.

options.label: D
options.text: Replicating the dataset to Amazon S3 so queries can be served from object storage instead of memory
options.rationale: Wrong. S3 is still a disk-backed file system and would not give interactive speed. The fitting solution keeps the dataset in distributed main memory after a single read.

questions.stem: A research team is building a Spark application that must analyze a large social network: they need to search for specific subgraphs (such as communities of mutually connected users) and compute PageRank scores to rank the most influential accounts. They want a Spark stack component purpose-built for this kind of graph computation rather than writing the algorithms from scratch on raw RDDs. Which component fits this requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: GraphX, the graph-processing library that provides operations such as subgraph searching and PageRank.
options.rationale: Correct. GraphX is the Spark stack component dedicated to graph processing, and the brief specifically lists subgraph searching and PageRank as the kinds of operations it supports, which is exactly what the team needs.

options.label: B
options.text: MLlib, since ranking influential accounts and finding communities are machine-learning clustering and classification tasks.
options.rationale: Incorrect. MLlib provides parallel machine-learning algorithms (preprocessing/dimensional reduction, classification, clustering), not graph-specific operations like subgraph search and PageRank. The graph-oriented requirement is served by GraphX, which is the correct answer.

options.label: C
options.text: Spark SQL, because graphs are stored as structured tables and can be traversed using its query optimizer.
options.rationale: Incorrect. Spark SQL is for querying structured datasets via SQL/Hive Query Language over sources like Hive tables, Parquet, and JSON; it is not designed for graph algorithms such as PageRank or subgraph searching. GraphX is the purpose-built graph component, making it the correct choice.

options.label: D
options.text: Spark Streaming, because the social network's connections arrive as a continuous live stream that must be processed in real time.
options.rationale: Incorrect. Spark Streaming processes live data streams in real time and operates on RDDs much like static RDDs; it does not provide graph algorithms. The task here is graph computation on a stored dataset, which is GraphX's role, so GraphX is correct.

questions.stem: A team is choosing between a traditional SQL database and a data lake for a new project. They want to load incoming files into storage right away without first defining any table structure, and then create tables on top of those files only later when someone needs to query them. Which storage approach matches this requirement, and what is the underlying principle?

options.label: A
options.text: A data lake, because it stores files first and applies table structure later when the data is queried (schema-on-read).
options.rationale: Correct. A data lake stores raw files first and lets tables be defined on top of them only at query time, which is exactly the schema-on-read principle the team wants.

options.label: B
options.text: A traditional SQL database, because it stores files first and lets you define tables afterward when querying (schema-on-read).
options.rationale: Wrong: a traditional SQL database uses schema-on-write, requiring a rigid table structure to be defined before any data can be inserted. The data lake, not the SQL database, is the system that stores files first and defines tables later.

options.label: C
options.text: A traditional SQL database, because it requires a fixed schema before insertion, which guarantees the files load immediately without any structure.
options.rationale: Wrong: requiring a fixed schema before insertion is the opposite of loading files immediately without structure. That schema-on-write requirement blocks the team's goal, whereas a data lake stores files first and defers the table definition.

options.label: D
options.text: A data lake, because it forces a schema to be defined and validated at write time before any file can be stored.
options.rationale: Wrong: forcing a schema at write time describes schema-on-write, which a data lake does not do. A data lake instead stores files first and applies structure later (schema-on-read), which is why it fits the requirement.

questions.stem: A product team is mapping workloads to NoSQL models for a new social platform. They need to store highly varied user-generated post formats (text, polls, embedded media with different fields) and, separately, to model and traverse the friend-connection network among millions of users. Which combination of NoSQL models best matches these two needs respectively?

options.label: A
options.text: Varied posts: column-family store; friend network: key-value store
options.rationale: Wrong. Column-family stores target columnar analytics, not varied document formats, and key-value stores cannot traverse a friend network. Varied posts fit document stores and the friend network fits a graph database.

options.label: B
options.text: Varied posts: document store; friend network: graph database
options.rationale: Correct. Varied, flexibly structured post formats map to document databases, while modeling and traversing friend connections maps to graph databases. This is the standard social-media matching.

options.label: C
options.text: Varied posts: graph database; friend network: document store
options.rationale: Wrong. The mapping is reversed. Graph databases are for traversing connections (the friend network), and document stores are for varied post formats. Swapping them mismatches each workload to the wrong model.

options.label: D
options.text: Varied posts: key-value store; friend network: column-family store
options.rationale: Wrong. Key-value stores cannot query inside varied post content, and column-family stores are for aggregation analytics, not relationship traversal. Varied posts fit document stores; the friend network fits a graph database.

questions.stem: A team is comparing a traditional SQL database with a data lake for a new project. In the SQL database, they must fully define every table's columns and data types before any record can be inserted. They want the opposite behavior: drop incoming data files into storage immediately and only define the table structure later, at the moment an analyst needs to query the data. Which pair of approaches correctly describes the SQL database versus the data lake, respectively?

options.label: A
options.text: The SQL database uses schema-on-write (structure defined before insertion); the data lake uses schema-on-read (files stored first, tables defined later on top of them).
options.rationale: Correct. A traditional SQL database has a rigid structure that must be defined before data is inserted, which is schema-on-write. A data lake stores files first and lets tables be created later on top of those files when a query is needed, which is schema-on-read, exactly the behavior the team wants.

options.label: B
options.text: The SQL database uses schema-on-read (files stored first); the data lake uses schema-on-write (structure defined before insertion).
options.rationale: Incorrect because the two labels are reversed. The rigid 'define columns and types before inserting' behavior is schema-on-write and belongs to the SQL database, while the 'store files now, define tables later' behavior is schema-on-read and belongs to the data lake, which is the opposite of what this option states.

options.label: C
options.text: Both the SQL database and the data lake use schema-on-write, but the data lake simply allows columns to be added faster.
options.rationale: Incorrect because a data lake does not use schema-on-write at all. It stores files first with no required up-front structure (schema-on-read), whereas the correct answer captures that only the SQL database requires the schema before insertion.

options.label: D
options.text: Both the SQL database and the data lake use schema-on-read, since neither requires a structure before storing data.
options.rationale: Incorrect because the SQL database does require a rigid structure before any record can be inserted, which is schema-on-write. Only the data lake is schema-on-read, so the correct answer distinguishes the two rather than treating them as identical.

questions.stem: A logistics company operates an online fraud-detection system that must inspect transaction events the moment they arrive from a live feed and flag suspicious ones within seconds, continuously and indefinitely. The team is already standardized on Spark and wants to reuse their existing RDD-based processing code rather than learn a completely different programming model. Which component of the Spark stack is purpose-built to handle this live, continuously arriving data while letting developers operate on it much like they already do with static RDDs?

options.label: A
options.text: Spark SQL, because it can run continuous SQL queries over structured data sources such as Hive tables, Parquet, and JSON.
options.rationale: Spark SQL is designed for interacting with structured datasets via SQL or querying APIs and uses a query optimizer over sources like Hive tables, Parquet, and JSON; it targets structured batch/interactive querying, not the ingestion and second-level processing of a continuous live event feed. The correct answer is Spark Streaming, which is specifically built to process live data streams in real time.

options.label: B
options.text: Spark Streaming, because it is designed to process live data streams in real time, and its APIs operate on RDDs in much the same way as on static RDDs.
options.rationale: Correct. Spark Streaming is the stack component built to process live data streams in real time, satisfying the requirement to inspect events as they arrive within seconds. Its APIs operate on RDDs similarly to static RDDs, which directly fulfills the team's goal of reusing their existing RDD-based code rather than adopting a new model.

options.label: C
options.text: MLlib, because it provides parallel machine-learning algorithms such as classification and clustering that can score incoming records.
options.rationale: MLlib is the machine-learning library offering parallel algorithms for preprocessing, classification, and clustering; while a fraud model might eventually use it for scoring, MLlib does not provide the mechanism for ingesting and processing a continuous live data feed in real time. That continuous-stream capability is exactly what Spark Streaming, the correct answer, provides.

options.label: D
options.text: GraphX, because real-time event flows are best modeled as graphs and processed with operations like subgraph searching and PageRank.
options.rationale: GraphX is the graph-processing library for operations such as subgraph searching and PageRank over graph-structured data; it is not built for ingesting and processing continuously arriving live data streams. The scenario's real-time stream-processing need is served by Spark Streaming, the correct answer, not by graph computation.

questions.stem: An enterprise architect is planning where to run a new Hadoop cluster. She wants to enumerate the legitimate deployment options for Apache Hadoop and the managed cloud offerings that can host it, so she can compare them in a proposal. All of the following are valid Hadoop deployment choices or managed cloud Hadoop services EXCEPT which one?

options.label: A
options.text: Running the cluster fully on-premises using the organization's own commodity servers in its data center.
options.rationale: This IS a valid choice. On-premises deployment on commodity hardware in the organization's own data center is one of the standard ways to run Hadoop, so it does not belong as the exception. The exception is the option that is not a real Hadoop deployment or managed service.

options.label: B
options.text: Using a managed cloud service such as Amazon EMR or Azure HDInsight to provision and operate the cluster.
options.rationale: This IS a valid choice. Amazon EMR and Azure HDInsight are recognized managed cloud services for running Hadoop, so it does not belong as the exception. The exception is the option that is not a genuine deployment or managed Hadoop offering.

options.label: C
options.text: Adopting a hybrid model that combines on-premises infrastructure with cloud resources for the cluster.
options.rationale: This IS a valid choice. Hybrid deployment that blends on-premises and cloud resources is a legitimate, commonly cited option, so it does not belong as the exception. The exception is the option that is not a real way to deploy or host Hadoop.

options.label: D
options.text: Deploying through 'Apache MapR-Direct,' a built-in single-node-only mode that intentionally disables HDFS replication and clustering.
options.rationale: This is the exception because it is fabricated. There is no Hadoop deployment option called 'Apache MapR-Direct,' and a mode that disables HDFS replication and clustering contradicts Hadoop's core design of distributed storage, three-times replication, and horizontal scalability. By contrast, on-premises, managed cloud services (Amazon EMR, Azure HDInsight), and hybrid deployments are all genuine, standard ways to run Hadoop.

questions.stem: A company must comply with regulations requiring it to control who can access which datasets, ensure data quality and usability, and enforce consistent policies across all data. A separate requirement is to protect data from unauthorized access and meet compliance obligations. Which pairing correctly maps these two requirements to the overarching architecture concerns, including an example tool?

options.label: A
options.text: Both requirements describe data ingestion, handled by Apache Kafka
options.rationale: Ingestion is about loading data, not enforcing access policies or protecting against unauthorized access. These requirements describe governance and security respectively.

options.label: B
options.text: The first describes security, the second describes governance, with Apache Atlas used for security
options.rationale: The labels are reversed: enforcing access/quality/usability policies is governance (Apache Atlas), and protecting against unauthorized access is security. This option swaps the two.

options.label: C
options.text: The first describes governance (enforcing rules/policies for access, quality, and usability, e.g., Apache Atlas), and the second describes security (protecting against unauthorized access and ensuring compliance)
options.rationale: Governance enforces rules and policies for data access, quality, and usability and is supported by Apache Atlas, while security protects against unauthorized access and ensures compliance, matching the two requirements.

options.label: D
options.text: Both requirements describe analytical sandboxing for exploratory analysis
options.rationale: Sandboxing is about isolated experimentation, not policy enforcement or access protection. The requirements correspond to governance and security.

questions.stem: A small team has no existing Hadoop or Mesos infrastructure but wants to stand up a Spark cluster quickly using only what ships with Spark itself. Which scheduler best fits this constraint?

options.label: A
options.text: Hadoop YARN, since it is bundled inside every Spark download
options.rationale: Wrong. YARN is the standard Hadoop scheduler and is not what ships inside Spark for this purpose. The included option is the Standalone Spark Scheduler.

options.label: B
options.text: A Mesos cluster, which Spark installs automatically on first launch
options.rationale: Wrong. Mesos is a separate cluster manager that Spark can use but does not auto-install. The scheduler included with Spark is the Standalone Spark Scheduler.

options.label: C
options.text: The Standalone Spark Scheduler, a simple cluster scheduler included with Spark
options.rationale: Correct. The brief describes the Standalone Spark Scheduler as a simple cluster scheduler included with Spark, ideal when no external manager exists.

options.label: D
options.text: The local scheduler, which manages multi-node clusters out of the box
options.rationale: Wrong. The local scheduler runs programs locally on a single PC, not across a multi-node cluster. For a small cluster with no external manager, the Standalone Spark Scheduler applies.

questions.stem: A retail chain implements a big data platform and, within a year, reports that managers make better-informed decisions, operations run more efficiently and productively, they understand their customers more deeply, and overall they spend less than with their old enterprise systems. These outcomes correspond to which category of big data benefits?

options.label: A
options.text: The core advantages of big data: enhanced decision-making, increased efficiency and productivity, enhanced customer insights, and cost-effectiveness
options.rationale: These four outcomes are exactly the recognized advantages of big data: better decisions, greater efficiency/productivity, deeper customer insight, and cost-effectiveness.

options.label: B
options.text: The 5 Vs of big data: Volume, Variety, Velocity, Veracity, and Value
options.rationale: The 5 Vs describe characteristics of the data itself, not business benefits realized after adoption. The outcomes listed are advantages, which is the correct category.

options.label: C
options.text: The architecture layers: ingestion, storage, analytics, and governance
options.rationale: Architecture layers describe how a platform is structured, not the benefits gained. Better decisions and cost savings are advantages, not layers.

options.label: D
options.text: The key drivers of big data: IoT growth, cheaper storage, and cloud computing
options.rationale: Key drivers are the forces that enabled big data to rise, not the outcomes a company experiences after adoption. The listed results are advantages.

questions.stem: An auditor asks a data team to reproduce exactly what a curated table looked like three weeks ago, before several rows were corrected, and also to guarantee that concurrent writes never leave the table half-updated. The data sits in object storage. Which capability set, provided by a lakehouse table format, directly addresses both requests?

options.label: A
options.text: Time travel/versioning for the historical snapshot, and ACID transactions to prevent half-updated states
options.rationale: Time travel/versioning lets the team query the table's earlier state, and ACID transactions ensure writes are atomic so concurrent updates never leave a partial state, addressing both auditor requests directly.

options.label: B
options.text: Partition pruning for the historical snapshot, and columnar compression to prevent half-updated states
options.rationale: Partition pruning only limits scanned data and does not recover past states, and compression does not provide transactional atomicity. Time travel and ACID transactions are the relevant capabilities.

options.label: C
options.text: Schema-on-read for the historical snapshot, and small-files compaction to prevent half-updated states
options.rationale: Schema-on-read defines structure at query time but cannot reproduce a prior version, and compaction addresses file counts, not write atomicity. The needed features are time travel/versioning and ACID transactions.

options.label: D
options.text: A data catalog for the historical snapshot, and JSON storage to prevent half-updated states
options.rationale: A catalog documents metadata but does not store historical table versions, and JSON offers no transactional guarantees. Time travel/versioning plus ACID transactions are what satisfy both requests.

questions.stem: A team compares two layouts for the same dataset: Layout X has 1,000,000 files of about 10 KB each; Layout Y has about 100 files of about 100 MB each. The total data size is identical. A distributed engine processes Layout Y far faster. Which principle does this outcome illustrate?

options.label: A
options.text: Because total size is equal, performance must be equal, so the observed difference is measurement error
options.rationale: Performance is not driven by total size alone; the number of files matters just as much. Layout X's million files create metadata overhead, genuinely making it slower.

options.label: B
options.text: In big data the total number of files affects engine performance as much as the total data size, so fewer larger files reduce metadata overhead
options.rationale: The golden rule is that the number of files impacts engine performance as much as the data's size. Layout Y's fewer, larger files minimize per-file metadata overhead and maximize throughput, explaining the speedup.

options.label: C
options.text: Layout Y is faster only because 100 MB files are automatically columnar while 10 KB files are not
options.rationale: File size does not change a file's format to columnar; the speedup comes from reduced per-file metadata overhead, not an automatic format change. The principle is the small-files problem.

options.label: D
options.text: Layout X is faster in theory; Layout Y only wins because of network caching unrelated to file count
options.rationale: Layout X is not faster; its million tiny files force the engine to open, list, and schedule each one, which is the real bottleneck. The advantage of Layout Y is fewer files reducing metadata overhead.

questions.stem: A team must store petabytes of data reliably across many commodity machines so that the failure of any single node does not lose data, and they want a distributed file system plus a processing framework that scales horizontally on inexpensive hardware. Apache Hadoop fits this need. Which of the following is NOT an accurate statement about Apache Hadoop?

options.label: A
options.text: Apache Hadoop provides distributed storage via HDFS and processing via MapReduce
options.rationale: This is an accurate statement: Hadoop pairs HDFS for distributed storage with MapReduce for processing, so it is not the exception the question seeks.

options.label: B
options.text: Hadoop is fault tolerant and designed to run on commodity hardware
options.rationale: This is accurate: Hadoop is fault tolerant and built to run on commodity hardware, so it does belong and is not the false statement.

options.label: C
options.text: Hadoop scales horizontally (scale-out) by adding more nodes to a cluster
options.rationale: This is accurate: Hadoop scales out horizontally by adding nodes, consistent with the big data approach, so it is not the exception.

options.label: D
options.text: Hadoop achieves its reliability by running on a single high-end vertically scaled server with no replication
options.rationale: This is the inaccurate statement and thus the correct choice: Hadoop relies on distributed nodes with built-in redundancy/replication and horizontal scaling, not a single vertically scaled server with no replication.

questions.stem: A company narrows its cloud strategy to the three major public cloud providers commonly used for big data workloads. A new hire incorrectly adds a fourth name to the list. Which of the following is NOT one of the major cloud providers named for big data?

options.label: A
options.text: Amazon Web Services (AWS)
options.rationale: AWS (Amazon) is one of the three major cloud providers named for big data, so it belongs and is not the exception.

options.label: B
options.text: Microsoft Azure
options.rationale: Microsoft Azure is one of the three major named cloud providers, so it belongs and is not the exception.

options.label: C
options.text: Apache Cassandra Cloud
options.rationale: This is the exception: Cassandra is a NoSQL database, not a major public cloud provider. The three named providers are AWS (Amazon), Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud.

options.label: D
options.text: Google Cloud
options.rationale: Google Cloud is one of the three major named cloud providers, so it belongs and is not the exception being sought.

questions.stem: A retailer stores a 200-column sales table. An analyst repeatedly runs SELECT AVG(total_amount) FROM sales over billions of rows. The platform team must minimize the I/O read for this query. Which storage layout will perform best and why?

options.label: A
options.text: Row-based storage such as CSV, because reading all 200 fields per row keeps the data physically contiguous and fast
options.rationale: Row-based storage forces the engine to read every field in every record, creating massive I/O overhead for a query that needs only one column. Columnar storage avoids reading the other 199 columns.

options.label: B
options.text: Columnar storage such as Parquet, because it can isolate and read only the total_amount column, bypassing the other 199 columns
options.rationale: Columnar storage isolates and extracts only the required column, completely bypassing irrelevant columns, which minimizes I/O for analytical queries that target a small fraction of columns.

options.label: C
options.text: Row-based storage, because columnar formats must always scan every column before computing an average
options.rationale: Columnar formats are designed to read only the needed columns, the opposite of scanning everything. Row-based storage is the one forced to read all fields, so it is slower here.

options.label: D
options.text: Either layout performs identically, because the average must touch all rows regardless of storage
options.rationale: Although all rows are involved, columnar storage reads only the single relevant column's values while row storage reads all 200 fields per row, so I/O differs dramatically. Columnar wins.

questions.stem: A media company runs a content management system where each article can have wildly different structures: some embed nested galleries, some have arrays of tags, and some omit fields entirely. Editors also need to run server-side queries that match articles based on values nested deep inside these structures. Which NoSQL model best satisfies both the flexible structure and the deep-query requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: Key-value store, because it stores each article under a unique key
options.rationale: Wrong. A key-value store can hold each article but cannot query inside the value; you can only retrieve by key. The requirement to match articles by deeply nested values needs a model that understands internal structure, i.e., a document store.

options.label: B
options.text: Document-oriented store, because it understands document structure and supports deep querying with flexible schemas
options.rationale: Correct. Document stores hold JSON/BSON/XML documents whose internal structure the database understands, enabling deep querying. They also offer ultimate schema flexibility (nested data, arrays, missing fields), matching both requirements exactly.

options.label: C
options.text: Column-family store, because columns can be sparse
options.rationale: Wrong. Sparse columns help analytics aggregation, but column-family stores are optimized for columnar aggregation, not deep querying of richly nested per-document structures. The flexible, nested, queryable documents described fit a document store.

options.label: D
options.text: Graph database, because articles are connected entities
options.rationale: Wrong. Graph databases are optimized for traversing relationships between nodes, not for storing and deeply querying self-contained, variably structured documents. The need here is flexible nested documents with deep querying, which is the document model.

questions.stem: A research team is building a data lake that must immediately accept incoming feeds whose structure is unknown and likely to change: today they receive flat CSV exports, but next month they expect nested JSON event logs and later raw image files. The team wants to ingest and store everything now and decide how to interpret each dataset's fields only later, at the moment they query it for a specific analysis. Which approach best fits this requirement, and why is it characteristic of a big data platform rather than a traditional system?

options.label: A
options.text: Schema-on-read, because the storage layer accepts data in its raw form and the structure is applied only when the data is queried, which suits big data's mix of evolving structured, semi-structured, and unstructured feeds.
options.rationale: Correct. Schema-on-read is the big data approach: data is stored in raw form and its structure is imposed at query time, which is exactly what the team needs to ingest unknown, changing formats now and interpret fields later. This flexibility is a defining contrast with the traditional schema-on-write model.

options.label: B
options.text: Schema-on-write, because defining a fixed schema before any data is loaded guarantees the feeds stay consistent as they evolve over time.
options.rationale: Wrong. Schema-on-write requires the schema to be defined before loading, which is the rigid traditional approach and would reject or break on the unknown, changing formats the team expects. The correct answer, schema-on-read, applies structure only at query time, accommodating evolving feeds.

options.label: C
options.text: Vertical scaling, because moving to a single larger server lets the platform store any format without needing to define structure in advance.
options.rationale: Wrong. Vertical scaling (scale-up) addresses capacity by enlarging one machine and says nothing about when structure is applied to data. The requirement is about deferring schema interpretation to query time, which is schema-on-read, not a scaling strategy.

options.label: D
options.text: Batch processing, because loading all feeds on a nightly schedule removes the need to know each dataset's structure ahead of time.
options.rationale: Wrong. Batch processing describes when data is processed (scheduled intervals), not how or when its structure is defined. The team's need to store raw data now and interpret fields later is the schema-on-read approach, independent of batch timing.

questions.stem: An airline analyst notes that a single commercial flight can generate roughly 10 TB of data every 30-minute interval and that there are about 22,000 commercial flights per day worldwide. The analyst wants to name the specific characteristic of big data that this observation most directly illustrates. Which of the 5 Vs is it?

options.label: A
options.text: Veracity, because flight data is always uncertain and noisy
options.rationale: Veracity concerns data quality, bias, noise, and reliability, not sheer quantity. The figures cited (terabytes per flight across thousands of flights) describe size, which is Volume.

options.label: B
options.text: Volume, because the figures describe the massive sheer size of data being accumulated
options.rationale: Volume refers to the massive size of data generated, such as transactions over years, social media, and sensor/M2M output; terabytes per flight multiplied across 22,000 daily flights is a textbook Volume example.

options.label: C
options.text: Value, because the data is clearly useful to the airline
options.rationale: Value is about the usefulness or business worth of data, not its quantity. The statistic given is strictly about how much data is produced, which is Volume.

options.label: D
options.text: Variety, because flights produce many different data formats
