options.rationale: Variety concerns the diversity of formats and sources. The cited statistic is purely about the quantity (terabytes) of data, which maps to Volume rather than format diversity.

questions.stem: A developer is storing customer records in a NoSQL document database. Some customers have a 'loyalty_tier' field, some have a 'referral_source' field, and many have neither, all within the same collection. No migration or table alteration is performed when a new field first appears on one record. Which core characteristic of NoSQL does this behavior demonstrate?

options.label: A
options.text: Shared-nothing architecture
options.rationale: Wrong. Shared-nothing refers to independent nodes that do not share memory or disk, enabling distribution and replication. The behavior described, records with differing fields in one collection, is about flexible structure, which is the schema-free characteristic.

options.label: B
options.text: Simple text-based API
options.rationale: Wrong. A simple API refers to low-level access via protocols like HTTP REST with JSON. Allowing heterogeneous fields within the same collection without migration is the schema-free characteristic, not the API style.

options.label: C
options.text: Schema-free (schema-on-read) data model allowing heterogeneous structures in the same domain
options.rationale: Correct. Storing records with differing and missing fields in the same collection without any migration is the schema-free trait: NoSQL applies schema-on-read and permits heterogeneous data structures within the same domain.

options.label: D
options.text: Strict referential integrity enforced through foreign keys
options.rationale: Wrong. Referential integrity via foreign keys is a relational feature that NoSQL specifically avoids. The scenario shows flexible, varying structure, which is schema-free behavior, the opposite of rigid integrity constraints.

questions.stem: A company runs a single powerful database server to process a growing dataset. As volume increases, performance degrades, and leadership debates two paths forward: one engineer proposes buying a single, far more powerful machine with faster CPUs and more RAM to replace the current server, while another proposes leaving the existing hardware in place and instead adding many additional commodity machines that work on the data in parallel. The team wants to follow the approach that aligns with the design philosophy behind frameworks like Hadoop. Which option correctly labels these two strategies and identifies the one Hadoop favors?

options.label: A
options.text: Replacing the server with a single faster machine is scale-out, and adding many commodity machines in parallel is scale-up; Hadoop favors scale-up.
options.rationale: The labels are reversed. Replacing one machine with a faster one is scale-UP, and adding many parallel nodes is scale-OUT. The correct answer keeps the definitions accurate: scale-out means adding more workers/nodes, which is the approach Hadoop favors via horizontal scalability on commodity hardware.

options.label: B
options.text: Replacing the server with a single faster machine is scale-up, and adding many commodity machines in parallel is scale-out; Hadoop favors scale-out.
options.rationale: Correct. Scale-up means replacing equipment with faster, more efficient hardware (vertical), while scale-out means adding more workers/nodes that process data in parallel (horizontal). Hadoop's core design principle of horizontal scalability favors scaling out across clusters of inexpensive commodity machines.

options.label: C
options.text: Both proposals are forms of scale-up because both aim to increase total processing capacity; Hadoop favors scale-up since a single coordinated machine is easier to manage.
options.rationale: This is wrong because the two proposals are fundamentally different strategies, not both scale-up. Adding parallel commodity nodes is scale-out, not scale-up, and Hadoop favors scale-out across many nodes rather than concentrating power in one easier-to-manage machine.

options.label: D
options.text: Replacing the server with a single faster machine is scale-out (distributing load to a stronger node), and Hadoop favors it because fewer machines reduce failure points.
options.rationale: This mislabels a single faster machine as scale-out and misstates Hadoop's preference. Upgrading one machine is scale-up, and Hadoop assumes hardware will fail, so it deliberately favors scale-out across many commodity nodes with replication rather than minimizing machine count.

questions.stem: A data scientist must run parallel versions of clustering and classification algorithms, plus some dimensional-reduction preprocessing, on a large distributed dataset. Which Spark stack component is purpose-built for these tasks?

options.label: A
options.text: MLlib
options.rationale: Correct. The brief describes MLlib as the machine-learning/data-mining library offering parallel versions of algorithms including preprocessing/dimensional reduction, classification, and clustering.

options.label: B
options.text: Spark SQL
options.rationale: Wrong. Spark SQL targets structured-data querying with SQL. Parallel clustering, classification, and dimensional reduction are provided by MLlib.

options.label: C
options.text: GraphFrames
options.rationale: Wrong. GraphFrames is a graph library based on DataFrames and Python. Machine-learning algorithms like clustering and classification come from MLlib.

options.label: D
options.text: Spark Core
options.rationale: Wrong. Spark Core provides task scheduling, memory management, fault recovery, and the RDD APIs, but not the ready-made ML algorithms; those are in MLlib, which sits on top of Spark Core.

questions.stem: A logistics company operates a fleet of delivery trucks that continuously transmit GPS coordinates and engine telemetry. The operations team wants to ingest this live feed and compute alerts (such as overheating or route deviation) within seconds of the data arriving, using the same RDD-style programming model their batch developers already know. Which component of the Spark stack is purpose-built to satisfy this requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: Spark SQL, because it can query continuously arriving structured data through SQL and Hive Query Language against Parquet and JSON sources
options.rationale: Wrong: Spark SQL is for interacting with structured datasets via SQL/querying APIs over sources like Hive tables, Parquet, and JSON using a query optimizer; it is not the component designed to process live data streams in real time. The correct answer is Spark Streaming, which is built to consume continuous live feeds and operate on them with the familiar RDD programming model.

options.label: B
options.text: Spark Streaming, because it processes live data streams in real time using APIs that operate on RDDs much like static RDDs
options.rationale: Correct: Spark Streaming is the stack component designed to process live data streams in real time, and its APIs operate on RDDs in a manner similar to static RDDs, which directly matches both the second-level latency requirement and the team's wish to reuse the RDD-style model they already know.

options.label: C
options.text: MLlib, because real-time alerting is fundamentally a classification problem and only the machine-learning library can react to streaming telemetry
options.rationale: Wrong: MLlib provides parallel versions of machine-learning algorithms (preprocessing/dimensional reduction, classification, clustering); it is not the component responsible for ingesting and processing a continuous live stream. While alerts could later use a model, the requirement here is real-time stream processing, which is exactly what Spark Streaming provides.

options.label: D
options.text: GraphX, because the truck routes form a network and graph processing is required to handle the incoming telemetry
options.rationale: Wrong: GraphX is a graph-processing library used for tasks like subgraph searching and PageRank, not for ingesting and reacting to continuous live data feeds. The scenario describes real-time stream processing on arriving telemetry, which is the role of Spark Streaming, not graph computation.

questions.stem: A company stores customer records in a traditional MySQL relational database and now wants to bulk-load that data into HDFS for analysis, with the ability to export results back to the relational system later. Which ecosystem tool is purpose-built for this RDBMS-to-Hadoop data integration?

options.label: A
options.text: Flume, because it is designed to transfer structured tables between relational databases and HDFS.
options.rationale: Flume is built for collecting and moving large log data into HDFS, not for relational table transfer. Sqoop is the tool designed for RDBMS-to-Hadoop integration.

options.label: B
options.text: Sqoop, which provides SQL-to-Hadoop data integration, importing data from an RDBMS into HDFS and exporting it back.
options.rationale: Sqoop (SQL-to-Hadoop) is purpose-built to transfer data between relational databases and Hadoop, importing into HDFS and exporting back, exactly the company's need.

options.label: C
options.text: Mahout, because it specializes in moving relational data into Hadoop for storage.
options.rationale: Mahout provides scalable machine-learning algorithms on Hadoop, not data transfer from databases. The relational import/export tool is Sqoop.

options.label: D
options.text: ZooKeeper, since it coordinates copying tables between databases and clusters.
options.rationale: ZooKeeper provides distributed coordination such as synchronization, naming, and configuration, not RDBMS data transfer. Sqoop handles relational-to-Hadoop movement.

questions.stem: A team tries to load yesterday's incoming product-feed files directly into their traditional SQL data warehouse, but the load job fails: several files contain new columns that suppliers added without warning, and a few records have a price field arriving as text instead of a number. The team observes that the warehouse refuses to accept any of the data until the table definition is updated to match. Which fundamental property of the traditional SQL warehouse explains this failure, and how would a data lake have avoided it?

options.label: A
options.text: The warehouse enforces schema-on-write, validating every record against a predefined table structure at load time; a data lake stores the files first and applies a schema only later when the data is read, so the mismatched files could be ingested immediately.
options.rationale: Correct. A traditional SQL warehouse requires the rigid table structure to be defined before insertion (schema-on-write), so incoming records that do not match are rejected at load time. A data lake follows schema-on-read: it stores the raw files as-is and defines tables on top of them only when an analyst queries, so the evolving, inconsistent files land successfully and are reconciled later.

options.label: B
options.text: The warehouse uses schema-on-read and therefore cannot validate incoming data, while a data lake uses schema-on-write to reject bad records before they ever land in storage.
options.rationale: Incorrect because it reverses the two models. It is the warehouse that uses schema-on-write (validating before insertion, hence the rejection), and the data lake that uses schema-on-read. The correct answer states the relationship the right way around: the warehouse's up-front validation caused the failure, and the lake's defer-the-schema approach would have accepted the files.

options.label: C
options.text: The load failed only because the files were stored as JSON rather than Parquet; converting them to a columnar binary format before loading would have let the warehouse accept the new columns and the text price automatically.
options.rationale: Incorrect because the file format is not the cause. The warehouse rejected the data because the records violated its predefined schema (new columns, a type mismatch), not because of row-vs-columnar encoding. Converting to Parquet does not relax schema enforcement. The correct answer identifies the real cause: schema-on-write validation, which a schema-on-read data lake avoids by storing files first.

options.label: D
options.text: The failure happened because the warehouse lacked a data catalog to document the new columns; adding an active catalog like AWS Glue would have let the load succeed without changing the table.
options.rationale: Incorrect because a catalog documents and helps users find and trust data; it does not override a warehouse's load-time schema validation. The records were rejected by schema-on-write enforcement, which a catalog cannot bypass. The correct answer points to that enforcement and to the data lake's schema-on-read model, which accepts the files first and defines structure later.

questions.stem: An on-premise data platform team is moving to the cloud. Today their files are split into fixed-size hardware blocks distributed across physical machines they maintain in a server room. They want to eliminate cluster hardware management while gaining near-infinite scalability, automated security, and native analytics integration. Which target best matches these goals?

options.label: A
options.text: Migrate to a larger HDFS cluster, since distributing blocks across more physical machines removes hardware management
options.rationale: HDFS is the legacy distributed file system that still requires managing physical on-premise machines and blocks, so a larger cluster increases rather than removes hardware management. Cloud object storage like S3 is what removes hardware management while adding scalability and native analytics.

options.label: B
options.text: Migrate to cloud object storage such as Amazon S3, which stores data as objects in scalable buckets with automated security and native analytics integration
options.rationale: Cloud object storage like Amazon S3 stores data as objects in highly scalable buckets, offers automated security, integrates natively with analytics, and removes the need to manage physical clusters.

options.label: C
options.text: Keep HDFS but add cloud buckets only for backups, since object storage cannot support analytics workloads
options.rationale: Object storage absolutely supports analytics; native analytics integration is one of its defining strengths. There is no need to retain HDFS for the primary store, so cloud object storage is the better target.

options.label: D
options.text: Adopt a single very large physical server with one giant disk so there are no blocks to distribute
options.rationale: A single physical server still requires hardware management and is not infinitely scalable. Cloud object storage is the approach that delivers scalability without managing physical machines.

questions.stem: A telemetry team ingests billions of time-stamped sensor readings and primarily runs analytical queries that compute SUM, COUNT, and AVG over a single measurement column across enormous ranges. They rarely update individual past records but value fast aggregation. Why is a column-family store a strong fit for this workload?

options.label: A
options.text: Because it physically stores a column's values contiguously on disk, making aggregations over that column extremely fast
options.rationale: Correct. Column-family stores organize data by columns and keep a single column's values contiguous on disk, which makes aggregation queries (SUM, COUNT, AVG) over that column extremely fast, ideal for analytics and time-series data.

options.label: B
options.text: Because it enforces ACID transactions across every row for analytical accuracy
options.rationale: Wrong. Column-family stores favor BASE-style guarantees and are actually weak at heavy transactional workloads. Their advantage here is contiguous columnar storage that accelerates aggregation, not ACID enforcement.

options.label: C
options.text: Because it traverses relationships between readings without expensive JOINs
options.rationale: Wrong. Relationship traversal without JOINs is the strength of graph databases. The column-family advantage for this aggregation-heavy workload is columnar, contiguous storage of values on disk.

options.label: D
options.text: Because it excels at frequent updates to individual records
options.rationale: Wrong. Updates to individual records are actually a weakness of column-family stores. Their strength, and the reason they fit this read/aggregation-heavy time-series workload, is fast columnar aggregation.

questions.stem: A logistics company is building a data pipeline that must ingest millions of raw event records per hour from hundreds of third-party carriers. Each carrier sends slightly different fields, and the formats change without notice. The team's top priority is to never reject or stall an incoming record just because its structure is unexpected; they are willing to deal with reconciling and interpreting the field differences later, at the moment they actually run reports. Which approach to schema handling best matches this requirement, and why?

options.label: A
options.text: A schema-on-write relational design, because validating every record against a predefined schema at ingestion guarantees the cleanest possible reports later.
options.rationale: Schema-on-write forces each record to conform to a rigid, predefined structure at the moment it is written, so records with unexpected fields would be rejected or would stall the pipeline. That directly violates the team's top priority of never blocking an incoming record. The correct answer is schema-on-read, which defers structural interpretation to query time and lets heterogeneous records land freely.

options.label: B
options.text: A schema-on-read NoSQL design, because it lets heterogeneous records with differing or missing fields be written without up-front validation, deferring interpretation of the structure until query time.
options.rationale: Correct. Schema-on-read is the defining NoSQL approach in which structure is not enforced at write time; records with varying or missing fields are stored as-is and the schema is applied (interpreted) only when the data is read for reporting. This exactly satisfies the requirement to never stall ingestion on unexpected structure while still allowing reconciliation later at query time.

options.label: C
options.text: A schema-on-write NoSQL design, because NoSQL systems validate the full structure of every document before accepting it, which prevents malformed carrier data from entering the store.
options.rationale: This mislabels NoSQL: NoSQL is schema-free and uses schema-on-read, not schema-on-write, so it does not validate a full predefined structure before accepting data. The premise that NoSQL blocks unexpected structure at write time is false, and such blocking would also break the requirement to never stall ingestion. The correct choice is the genuine schema-on-read approach.

options.label: D
options.text: Either approach works equally well, because both schema-on-write and schema-on-read enforce structure at ingestion and merely differ in storage format.
options.rationale: This is wrong because the two approaches are not equivalent: schema-on-write enforces structure at ingestion while schema-on-read defers it to query time, and that timing difference is the whole point. Only schema-on-read avoids stalling on unexpected fields, so the approaches are not interchangeable for this requirement.

questions.stem: A team must choose a partition column for a transactions data lake. Queries almost always filter by transaction date and occasionally by region. An engineer proposes partitioning by transaction_id because it is unique and indexed-looking. What is the most likely consequence of partitioning by transaction_id, and what should they do instead?

options.label: A
options.text: It will speed up date queries; they should keep transaction_id and add date as a secondary partition
options.rationale: Partitioning by transaction_id does not help date queries and creates a folder per transaction. The proper fix is to partition by date (and possibly region), the columns actually filtered on.

options.label: B
options.text: It will create an enormous number of tiny folders (one per transaction), and they should instead partition by date and possibly region
options.rationale: Partitioning by a unique identifier produces too many tiny folders, hurting performance. Best practice is to partition by frequently filtered, low-cardinality columns like date or region.

options.label: C
options.text: It will have no effect on performance, so the choice between transaction_id and date is irrelevant
options.rationale: The choice is far from irrelevant; a unique-id partition creates excessive folders and harms performance, while date/region partitioning enables effective pruning.

options.label: D
options.text: It will enable partition pruning on date queries automatically, since every value is distinct
options.rationale: Distinct values do not help pruning on date filters; queries filter on date, not transaction_id, so no pruning benefit results. Partitioning by the actually-filtered date column is what enables pruning.

questions.stem: A team summarizes the design goals that motivated Spark for big-data processing. All of the following are stated goals of Spark EXCEPT which one?

options.label: A
options.text: Guaranteeing exactly-once delivery of network packets between worker nodes
options.rationale: Correct (this is the EXCEPTION). Exactly-once packet delivery is not a stated Spark goal. The documented goals are generality, low latency, fault tolerance, and simplicity.

options.label: B
options.text: Generality, supporting diverse workloads, operators, and job sizes
options.rationale: This IS a stated goal. The brief lists generality (diverse workloads, operators, job sizes) as a core Spark goal, so it is not the exception.

options.label: C
options.text: Low latency, with sub-second response
options.rationale: This IS a stated goal. The brief lists low latency (sub-second) as a Spark goal, so it does belong and is not the exception.

options.label: D
options.text: Fault tolerance, treating faults as the norm rather than the exception
options.rationale: This IS a stated goal. The brief lists fault tolerance, noting faults are the norm not the exception, so it belongs and is not the exception.

questions.stem: A healthcare analytics firm is mapping its biggest risks before launch. It worries specifically about an algorithm producing biased outcomes against certain patient groups, disputes over who owns patient-generated data, and the obligation to deploy AI responsibly. According to the standard categorization of big data challenges, into which category do these three concerns fall?

options.label: A
options.text: Technical challenges
options.rationale: Technical challenges involve scalability, storage management, real-time processing, and system reliability. Bias, data ownership, and responsible AI are ethical challenges, not technical ones.

options.label: B
options.text: Ethical challenges
options.rationale: Algorithmic bias, data ownership, and responsible AI are precisely the ethical challenges of big data, matching all three concerns the firm lists.

options.label: C
options.text: Data challenges
options.rationale: Data challenges concern quality, cleaning, integration from multiple sources, and lifecycle/governance. The firm's concerns about bias and ownership are ethical, not data-quality, issues.

options.label: D
options.text: Security and privacy challenges
options.rationale: Security and privacy challenges cover breaches, encryption, and compliance such as GDPR. Algorithmic bias and responsible AI fall under ethics, which is the correct category.

questions.stem: A video-sharing company runs its catalog on one powerful database server. As uploads surge into the petabyte range, the server keeps hitting its limits, and engineers find there is no bigger single machine they can buy to keep up. They decide to spread the workload across a large cluster of inexpensive commodity machines, adding more nodes as demand grows. Which scaling approach are they adopting, and how does it differ from what they were doing before?

options.label: A
options.text: They are adopting horizontal scaling (scale-out) by adding more commodity nodes to a cluster, whereas before they relied on vertical scaling (scale-up) by upgrading a single server.
options.rationale: Correct. Spreading work across many commodity machines and adding nodes as demand grows is horizontal scaling (scale-out), the big data approach. Their original strategy of running on one powerful server and upgrading it is vertical scaling (scale-up), the traditional approach that hits a ceiling when no larger single machine exists.

options.label: B
options.text: They are adopting vertical scaling (scale-up) across the cluster, whereas before they used horizontal scaling (scale-out) on one server.
options.rationale: Incorrect. This reverses the two terms. Vertical scaling means upgrading a single machine (what they did before and could no longer do), while distributing across many nodes is horizontal scaling (scale-out), which is what they are now adopting.

options.label: C
options.text: They are switching from schema-on-read to schema-on-write, which lets them use more machines.
options.rationale: Incorrect. Schema-on-read versus schema-on-write describes when data structure is applied, not how compute is distributed. The scenario is about distributing load across machines, which is horizontal scaling (scale-out), not a schema strategy.

options.label: D
options.text: They are replacing batch processing with real-time streaming, which removes the need for a bigger server.
options.rationale: Incorrect. Batch versus streaming describes when and how data is processed, not how hardware capacity is expanded. The company's change is about spreading load over many commodity nodes, which is horizontal scaling (scale-out).

questions.stem: A team is choosing between a traditional SQL database and a data lake for a new project. A senior engineer notes one key operational difference: in a traditional SQL database, the table structure (column names and types) must be fully defined and enforced at the moment data is written, whereas the data lake lets them store raw files now and impose a table structure only when someone runs a query later. What are these two approaches respectively called?

options.label: A
options.text: Schema-on-write for the SQL database, and schema-on-read for the data lake.
options.rationale: A traditional SQL database enforces a rigid structure at insertion time (schema-on-write), while a data lake stores files first and applies a table structure only when the data is queried (schema-on-read). This matches the operational difference the engineer described.

options.label: B
options.text: Schema-on-read for the SQL database, and schema-on-write for the data lake.
options.rationale: This reverses the two terms. The SQL database is the one that enforces structure at write time (schema-on-write), and the data lake defers structure to query time (schema-on-read), so the labels are swapped here.

options.label: C
options.text: Partition pruning for the SQL database, and small-files batching for the data lake.
options.rationale: These are query-optimization and file-layout concepts, not descriptions of when a schema is enforced. The actual distinction the engineer described is schema-on-write versus schema-on-read.

options.label: D
options.text: ACID compliance for the SQL database, and the Medallion architecture for the data lake.
options.rationale: ACID is a transactional reliability property and Medallion is a zone-layering pattern; neither names the timing of schema enforcement. The correct contrast for enforcing structure at write time versus at read time is schema-on-write versus schema-on-read.

questions.stem: A NoSQL evangelist lists what makes the 'core anatomy' of NoSQL databases distinct from relational systems. All of the following are part of that core anatomy EXCEPT which one?

options.label: A
options.text: Non-relational design working with self-contained aggregates or BLOBs instead of complex referential-integrity joins
options.rationale: This IS part of NoSQL's core anatomy. Being non-relational, favoring self-contained aggregates over join-heavy referential integrity, is a defining trait, so it is not the exception.

options.label: B
options.text: A simple, text-based API (often HTTP REST with JSON) for low-level data manipulation
options.rationale: This IS part of NoSQL's core anatomy. A simple API using text-based protocols like HTTP REST with JSON is a defining trait, so it is not the exception.

options.label: C
options.text: Mandatory enforcement of a rigid, predefined schema before any data can be written
options.rationale: Correct (the exception). NoSQL is schema-free (schema-on-read), not schema-on-write. Requiring a rigid predefined schema before writing is a relational trait, the opposite of NoSQL's anatomy, making it the option that does NOT belong.

options.label: D
options.text: Distributed, shared-nothing architecture with peer-to-peer multi-master replication
options.rationale: This IS part of NoSQL's core anatomy. A distributed, shared-nothing design with peer-to-peer multi-master replication is a defining trait, so it is not the exception.

questions.stem: A logistics company has set up sensors on its delivery trucks that continuously emit location and temperature readings. The data engineering team needs to process this constant flow of incoming data as it arrives so a dashboard can update in near real time, and they want to keep using the same RDD-based programming style their batch jobs already use. Which component of the Spark stack is purpose-built for this requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: Spark SQL, because it converts the incoming readings into structured tables and runs an optimizer over them.
options.rationale: Spark SQL is for interacting with structured datasets using SQL or querying APIs over data sources like Hive tables, Parquet, and JSON, using a query optimizer. It is not designed to process a continuous live stream of arriving data, which is what the company needs; that role belongs to Spark Streaming, whose APIs operate on RDDs much like static RDDs.

options.label: B
options.text: Spark Streaming, because it is designed to process live data streams in real time with APIs that operate on RDDs much like static RDDs.
options.rationale: Spark Streaming is the component built specifically to process live data streams in real time, and its APIs operate on RDDs in a way similar to static RDDs. This matches both the continuous near-real-time processing need and the team's desire to keep their familiar RDD-based programming style.

options.label: C
options.text: MLlib, because the continuously arriving sensor data must first be passed through a clustering algorithm before it can be displayed.
options.rationale: MLlib is the machine-learning/data-mining library offering parallel versions of algorithms such as clustering, classification, and dimensional-reduction preprocessing. The scenario only requires ingesting and processing a live stream for a dashboard, not running ML algorithms; the streaming ingestion and real-time processing is the job of Spark Streaming.

options.label: D
options.text: Spark Core, because real-time stream processing is one of the foundational services it provides directly to applications.
options.rationale: Spark Core provides the foundational services every component relies on (task scheduling, memory management, fault recovery, and the APIs to create RDDs and apply transformations/actions), but it does not itself provide real-time stream processing. That specialized capability is supplied by the higher-level Spark Streaming component that sits on top of Spark Core.

questions.stem: A team is building a global notifications feed where staying online and accepting writes during regional network hiccups matters more than every region showing identical data at the exact same instant. They are told their datastore follows the BASE model rather than strict ACID. In practice, what should the team expect the BASE approach to provide for this feed?

options.label: A
options.text: The system stays basically available and accepts writes, allowing nodes to temporarily hold differing (soft) state that converges to a consistent view over time (eventual consistency).
options.rationale: Correct. BASE stands for Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency. It keeps the system available and accepting requests during disruptions, tolerates temporarily inconsistent (soft) state across nodes, and guarantees the data converges to a consistent view eventually, which is exactly the trade-off this feed wants.

options.label: B
options.text: Every read across all regions is guaranteed to reflect the most recent committed write the instant it commits, with no possibility of stale data.
options.rationale: Wrong. That instantaneous, identical-everywhere guarantee is strict consistency (the C in ACID and CAP), which is the philosophy BASE deliberately relaxes. BASE instead accepts temporarily stale data that converges later, which is what the correct answer describes.

options.label: C
options.text: Transactions are fully atomic, isolated, and durable across all regions, so partial or uncommitted writes can never be seen anywhere.
options.rationale: Wrong. Full atomicity, isolation, and durability with no partial visibility describes ACID compliance, the model typical of relational systems. BASE relaxes these strict guarantees in favor of availability and eventual consistency, as the correct answer states.

options.label: D
options.text: The system blocks all reads and writes during a network disruption until every node can be confirmed in sync, preserving a single global truth.
options.rationale: Wrong. Blocking access until all nodes are confirmed in sync is the consistency-favoring behavior of a CP-style system, the opposite of BASE's goal of staying available. BASE keeps serving requests and lets state converge afterward, which the correct answer captures.

questions.stem: A Hadoop cluster is spread across multiple racks in a data center, organized in a tree-style master-slave topology. A job scheduler must decide where to place a task that processes a particular block. Considering how network bandwidth varies with node location, which placement reasoning is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Placement should ignore rack location entirely, since all node-to-node bandwidth in a data center is identical regardless of position.
options.rationale: Bandwidth depends on relative location: same-rack nodes communicate faster than off-rack nodes. Ignoring rack topology would waste the locality advantage that the design exploits.

options.label: B
options.text: Placing the task on a node holding the block, or failing that on a same-rack node, is preferable because nodes within the same rack have higher bandwidth than off-rack nodes.
options.rationale: In the tree-style rack topology, same-rack nodes have higher bandwidth than off-rack nodes, so running the task on the data-holding node or a same-rack node minimizes data transfer cost, consistent with data locality.

options.label: C
options.text: Placement should always pick a node on a different rack to balance load, since cross-rack links are the fastest in the cluster.
options.rationale: Cross-rack (off-rack) links have lower bandwidth than same-rack links, so deliberately choosing a different rack is slower. Same-rack or on-node placement is preferred.

options.label: D
options.text: All tasks must run only on the master node, since slaves cannot reach the data blocks over the rack network.
options.rationale: Tasks run on slave nodes near the data, exploiting rack-aware locality; the master coordinates rather than executing all tasks. Slaves do reach blocks, and same-rack placement is favored for bandwidth.

questions.stem: In a PySpark program a developer builds linesRDD with sc.textFile(inputFile) so each element is a line, then calls numLines = linesRDD.count() and prints numLines. Considering the difference between transformations and actions, what role does count() play here?

options.label: A
options.text: count() is a transformation that builds a new RDD of line counts without returning a value
options.rationale: Wrong. count() is an action, not a transformation. It returns a single value (the number of lines) to the driver rather than building a new RDD.

options.label: B
options.text: count() is the call that establishes the connection to the cluster
options.rationale: Wrong. The connection to the cluster is established by creating the SparkContext, not by count(). count() is an action that returns a value to the driver.

options.label: C
options.text: count() is an action that triggers computation and returns a value to the driver
options.rationale: Correct. The brief states count() is an action returning a value to the driver; actions trigger computation, unlike transformations that merely build RDDs.

options.label: D
options.text: count() reads the input file and constructs the RDD, replacing the need for sc.textFile
options.rationale: Wrong. sc.textFile builds the RDD (each element a line); count() then acts on that RDD as an action returning a value. count() does not read the file in place of textFile.
